== History == [[File:Shinodayama maneuvering ground Aerial photograph.1985.jpg|thumb|300px|Aerial photo of the Shinodayama Training Ground area, created by combining eight images taken in 1985. {{ill|Kokudo Kōkū Shashin|ja|国土航空写真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokudo Kōkū Shashin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491397}}.]]
Based on the presence of {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} kiln remains in the [[:en:Senboku|Senboku]] region, it is believed that pine forests existed in the Shinodayama Hills around the {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century}}. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the area, known as Shinodayama within the precincts of {{ill|Hijiri Shrine (Izumi)|ja|聖神社 (和泉市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiri Shrine (Izumi)|1=Hijiri Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611103}}, was managed by the shrine.
In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the government confiscated Shinodayama under the [[Joshi Rei]] decree, and it became state land under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sakai Prefecture|Sakai Prefecture]] in {{ill|1870|en|1870|WD=Q7741|lt=1870}} (Meiji 3). Under Sakai Prefecture, new rice fields were developed, and the area became known as Onoshinden after the Ono family, who led the development. Onoshinden engaged in sericulture, but typhoon damage and water shortages led to its abandonment by around {{ill|1885|en|1885|WD=Q7821|lt=1885}} (Meiji 18). Meanwhile, by {{ill|1873|en|1873|WD=Q7757|lt=1873}} (Meiji 6), an artillery shooting range had been established, which was later designated as the '''Shinodayama Army Training Ground'''.<ref name="global-kansai">{{Cite web|format=PDF|url=http://www.global-kansai.or.jp/bukai/img/H25.1.28-2.pdf|title=Civil Movements to Protect Biodiversity: The Plan for a Satoyama Nature Park in the Shinodayama Hills|author=NPO Shinoda no Mori Fan Club|publisher=Global Environmental Forum Kansai|archivedate=2016-03-04}}</ref>
The training ground was used by units under the {{ill|4th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|4th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|WD=Q1305480|lt=4th Division}} headquartered at {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Osaka Castle}}. In {{ill|1919|en|1919|WD=Q2157|lt=1919}} (Taisho 8), the {{ill|4th Field Artillery Regiment|en|4th Field Artillery Regiment|WD=Q4639267|lt=4th Field Artillery Regiment}}<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.mod.go.jp/gsdf/mae/3d/shinodayama/gaiyou.html|title=Overview of Shinodayama Garrison|publisher=[[Ministry of Defense, Japan]]|accessdate=2017-11-09}}</ref> moved to the area, establishing a large military base.
Following {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, [[Shinodayama Garrison]] became home to the {{ill|27th Infantry Regiment (United States)|en|27th Infantry Regiment (United States)|WD=Q4632617|lt=27th Infantry Regiment}} of the {{ill|25th Infantry Division (United States)|en|25th Infantry Division (United States)|WD=Q217777|lt=25th Infantry Division}}, part of the American {{ill|Occupation of Japan|en|Occupation of Japan|WD=Q696251|lt=occupation forces}}. This regiment later deployed to the {{ill|Korean War|en|Korean War|WD=Q8663|lt=Korean War}}, where its training at Shinodayama contributed to its effectiveness.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=The Korean War's Early Challenges and the Role of Japanese Facilities|journal=Military Research|issue=June 2013}}</ref>
After the U.S. withdrawal, the [[:en:JGSDF|JGSDF]] established the Shinodayama Garrison in {{ill|1957|en|1957|WD=Q5311|lt=1957}} (Showa 32). The training ground has since been used concurrently with the garrison. From {{ill|1997|en|1997|WD=Q2088|lt=1997}} (Heisei 9), the Ministry of Defense began acquiring privately owned land, culminating in the purchase of 16 hectares by the Izumi City Land Development Corporation in {{ill|2004|en|2004|WD=Q2014|lt=2004}} (Heisei 16).<ref name="global-kansai"/>
Since {{ill|2003|en|2003|WD=Q1986|lt=2003}} (Heisei 15), the {{ill|Shinodayama Cross-Country Race|ja|信太山クロスカントリー大会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinodayama Cross-Country Race|1=Shinodayama Cross-Country Race|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48755990}} has been held at the training ground.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/kakukano/syougaibu/sportssinkou/osirase/kurosukantori_taikai/1321680627212.html|title=History of the Shinodayama Cross-Country Race|publisher=[[Izumi City]]|accessdate=2017-11-09}}</ref>
== Current State == The Shinodayama Hills were once covered in pine forests, but deforestation for fuel and [[:en:pine wilt disease|pine wilt disease]] have reduced vegetation. The soil is highly acidic, making it less suitable for agriculture but ideal for {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} production.<ref name="Izumi City">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/7/02rekishi.pdf|title=Historical Overview of the Shinodayama Hills|publisher=Izumi City|format=PDF|accessdate=2017-11-09}}</ref>
The training ground spans approximately 2.2 million square meters, with about 27,000 square meters of privately owned land. It features numerous reservoirs, including the large Ōtani Reservoir. The area also includes a bypass road with covered firing ranges and remnants of a former vehicle training facility.
The surrounding area is primarily residential, with nearby parks, cemeteries, agricultural fields, and industrial zones. Despite development, the training ground preserves rare natural habitats, leading to conservation efforts by Izumi City and local citizens.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/7/02gijiroku.pdf|title=2nd Meeting Summary on Shinodayama Hills Conservation|publisher=Izumi City|format=PDF|accessdate=2017-11-09}}</ref>
In the late 1990s, sections of the training ground were affected by illegal industrial waste dumping. Cleanup efforts began in {{ill|2008|en|2008|WD=Q2004|lt=2008}} (Heisei 20), funded by the management of Ōtani Reservoir.<ref name="泉州">{{Cite web|url=http://www.sen-shu.com/net/news/news.cgi?mode=print&type=senshu2&no=4545|title=Shinodayama Training Ground Waste Cleanup Begins|publisher=Senshu News|archivedate=2014-02-22}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[List of Japan Ground Self-Defense Force training grounds]] * {{ill|Shinodayama Discrimination Incident|ja|信太山自衛隊差別事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinodayama Discrimination Incident|1=Shinodayama Discrimination Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386021}}

== History == [[File:Shinodayama maneuvering ground Aerial photograph.1985.jpg|thumb|300px|Aerial photo of the Shinodayama Training Ground area, created by combining eight images taken in 1985. {{ill|Kokudo Kōkū Shashin|ja|国土航空写真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokudo Kōkū Shashin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491397}}.]]
Based on the presence of {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} kiln remains in the [[:en:Senboku|Senboku]] region, it is believed that pine forests existed in the Shinodayama Hills around the {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century}}. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the area, known as Shinodayama within the precincts of {{ill|Hijiri Shrine (Izumi)|ja|聖神社 (和泉市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiri Shrine (Izumi)|1=Hijiri Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611103}}, was managed by the shrine.
In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the government confiscated Shinodayama under the [[Joshi Rei]] decree, and it became state land under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sakai Prefecture|Sakai Prefecture]] in {{ill|1870|en|1870|WD=Q7741|lt=1870}} (Meiji 3). Under Sakai Prefecture, new rice fields were developed, and the area became known as Onoshinden after the Ono family, who led the development. Onoshinden engaged in sericulture, but typhoon damage and water shortages led to its abandonment by around {{ill|1885|en|1885|WD=Q7821|lt=1885}} (Meiji 18). Meanwhile, by {{ill|1873|en|1873|WD=Q7757|lt=1873}} (Meiji 6), an artillery shooting range had been established, which was later designated as the '''Shinodayama Army Training Ground'''.<ref name="global-kansai">{{Cite web|format=PDF|url=http://www.global-kansai.or.jp/bukai/img/H25.1.28-2.pdf|title=Civil Movements to Protect Biodiversity: The Plan for a Satoyama Nature Park in the Shinodayama Hills|author=NPO Shinoda no Mori Fan Club|publisher=Global Environmental Forum Kansai|archivedate=2016-03-04}}</ref>
The training ground was used by units under the {{ill|4th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|4th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|WD=Q1305480|lt=4th Division}} headquartered at {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Osaka Castle}}. In {{ill|1919|en|1919|WD=Q2157|lt=1919}} (Taisho 8), the {{ill|4th Field Artillery Regiment|en|4th Field Artillery Regiment|WD=Q4639267|lt=4th Field Artillery Regiment}}<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.mod.go.jp/gsdf/mae/3d/shinodayama/gaiyou.html|title=Overview of Shinodayama Garrison|publisher=[[Ministry of Defense, Japan]]|accessdate=2017-11-09}}</ref> moved to the area, establishing a large military base.
Following {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, [[Shinodayama Garrison]] became home to the {{ill|27th Infantry Regiment (United States)|en|27th Infantry Regiment (United States)|WD=Q4632617|lt=27th Infantry Regiment}} of the {{ill|25th Infantry Division (United States)|en|25th Infantry Division (United States)|WD=Q217777|lt=25th Infantry Division}}, part of the American {{ill|Occupation of Japan|en|Occupation of Japan|WD=Q696251|lt=occupation forces}}. This regiment later deployed to the {{ill|Korean War|en|Korean War|WD=Q8663|lt=Korean War}}, where its training at Shinodayama contributed to its effectiveness.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=The Korean War's Early Challenges and the Role of Japanese Facilities|journal=Military Research|issue=June 2013}}</ref>
After the U.S. withdrawal, the [[:en:JGSDF|JGSDF]] established the Shinodayama Garrison in {{ill|1957|en|1957|WD=Q5311|lt=1957}} (Showa 32). The training ground has since been used concurrently with the garrison. From {{ill|1997|en|1997|WD=Q2088|lt=1997}} (Heisei 9), the Ministry of Defense began acquiring privately owned land, culminating in the purchase of 16 hectares by the Izumi City Land Development Corporation in {{ill|2004|en|2004|WD=Q2014|lt=2004}} (Heisei 16).<ref name="global-kansai"/>
Since {{ill|2003|en|2003|WD=Q1986|lt=2003}} (Heisei 15), the {{ill|Shinodayama Cross-Country Race|ja|信太山クロスカントリー大会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinodayama Cross-Country Race|1=Shinodayama Cross-Country Race|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48755990}} has been held at the training ground.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/kakukano/syougaibu/sportssinkou/osirase/kurosukantori_taikai/1321680627212.html|title=History of the Shinodayama Cross-Country Race|publisher=[[Izumi City]]|accessdate=2017-11-09}}</ref>
== Current State == The Shinodayama Hills were once covered in pine forests, but deforestation for fuel and [[:en:pine wilt disease|pine wilt disease]] have reduced vegetation. The soil is highly acidic, making it less suitable for agriculture but ideal for {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} production.<ref name="Izumi City">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/7/02rekishi.pdf|title=Historical Overview of the Shinodayama Hills|publisher=Izumi City|format=PDF|accessdate=2017-11-09}}</ref>
The training ground spans approximately 2.2 million square meters, with about 27,000 square meters of privately owned land. It features numerous reservoirs, including the large Ōtani Reservoir. The area also includes a bypass road with covered firing ranges and remnants of a former vehicle training facility.
The surrounding area is primarily residential, with nearby parks, cemeteries, agricultural fields, and industrial zones. Despite development, the training ground preserves rare natural habitats, leading to conservation efforts by Izumi City and local citizens.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/7/02gijiroku.pdf|title=2nd Meeting Summary on Shinodayama Hills Conservation|publisher=Izumi City|format=PDF|accessdate=2017-11-09}}</ref>
In the late 1990s, sections of the training ground were affected by illegal industrial waste dumping. Cleanup efforts began in {{ill|2008|en|2008|WD=Q2004|lt=2008}} (Heisei 20), funded by the management of Ōtani Reservoir.<ref name="泉州">{{Cite web|url=http://www.sen-shu.com/net/news/news.cgi?mode=print&type=senshu2&no=4545|title=Shinodayama Training Ground Waste Cleanup Begins|publisher=Senshu News|archivedate=2014-02-22}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[List of Japan Ground Self-Defense Force training grounds]] * {{ill|Shinodayama Discrimination Incident|ja|信太山自衛隊差別事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinodayama Discrimination Incident|1=Shinodayama Discrimination Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386021}}

{{Coord|34.492003|135.461286|format=dms|display=title}} '''Shinodayama Enshūjō''' (信太山演習場) is a {{ill|Japan Ground Self-Defense Force|en|Japan Ground Self-Defense Force|WD=Q889751|lt=Japan Ground Self-Defense Force|1=Japan Ground Self-Defense Force}} ([[:en:JGSDF|JGSDF]]) training ground located in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|en|Izumi, Osaka|WD=Q696412|lt=Izumi City|1=Izumi, Osaka}} and partly extending into [[:en:Sakai City|Sakai City]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. The area, including the training ground, is also referred to as the '''Shinodayama Hills'''.
== History == [[File:Shinodayama maneuvering ground Aerial photograph.1985.jpg|thumb|300px|Aerial photo of the Shinodayama Training Ground area, created by combining eight images taken in 1985. {{ill|Kokudo Kōkū Shashin|ja|国土航空写真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokudo Kōkū Shashin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491397|1=Geospatial Information Authority of Japan}}.]]
Based on the presence of {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} kiln remains in the [[:en:Senboku|Senboku]] region, it is believed that pine forests existed in the Shinodayama Hills around the {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}}. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the area, known as Shinodayama within the precincts of {{ill|Hijiri Shrine (Izumi)|ja|聖神社 (和泉市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiri Shrine (Izumi)|1=Hijiri Shrine (Q11611103)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611103}}, was managed by the shrine.
In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the government confiscated Shinodayama under the [[Joshi Rei]] decree, and it became state land under the jurisdiction of [[:en:Sakai Prefecture|Sakai Prefecture]] in {{ill|1870|en|1870|WD=Q7741|lt=1870|1=1870}} (Meiji 3). Under Sakai Prefecture, new rice fields were developed, and the area became known as Onoshinden after the Ono family, who led the development. Onoshinden engaged in sericulture, but typhoon damage and water shortages led to its abandonment by around {{ill|1885|en|1885|WD=Q7821|lt=1885|1=1885}} (Meiji 18). Meanwhile, by {{ill|1873|en|1873|WD=Q7757|lt=1873|1=1873}} (Meiji 6), an artillery shooting range had been established, which was later designated as the '''Shinodayama Army Training Ground'''.<ref name="global-kansai">{{Cite web|format=PDF|url=http://www.global-kansai.or.jp/bukai/img/H25.1.28-2.pdf|title=Civil Movements to Protect Biodiversity: The Plan for a Satoyama Nature Park in the Shinodayama Hills|author=NPO Shinoda no Mori Fan Club|publisher=Global Environmental Forum Kansai|archivedate=2016-03-04}}</ref>
The training ground was used by units under the {{ill|4th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|4th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|WD=Q1305480|lt=4th Division|1=4th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)}} headquartered at {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Osaka Castle|1=Osaka Castle}}. In {{ill|1919|en|1919|WD=Q2157|lt=1919|1=1919}} (Taisho 8), the {{ill|4th Field Artillery Regiment|en|4th Field Artillery Regiment|WD=Q4639267|lt=4th Field Artillery Regiment|1=4th Field Artillery Regiment}}<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.mod.go.jp/gsdf/mae/3d/shinodayama/gaiyou.html|title=Overview of Shinodayama Garrison|publisher=[[Ministry of Defense, Japan]]|accessdate=2017-11-09}}</ref> moved to the area, establishing a large military base.
Following {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, [[Shinodayama Garrison]] became home to the {{ill|27th Infantry Regiment (United States)|en|27th Infantry Regiment (United States)|WD=Q4632617|lt=27th Infantry Regiment|1=27th Infantry Regiment (United States)}} of the {{ill|25th Infantry Division (United States)|en|25th Infantry Division (United States)|WD=Q217777|lt=25th Infantry Division|1=25th Infantry Division (United States)}}, part of the American {{ill|Occupation of Japan|en|Occupation of Japan|WD=Q696251|lt=occupation forces|1=Occupation of Japan}}. This regiment later deployed to the {{ill|Korean War|en|Korean War|WD=Q8663|lt=Korean War|1=Korean War}}, where its training at Shinodayama contributed to its effectiveness.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=The Korean War's Early Challenges and the Role of Japanese Facilities|journal=Military Research|issue=June 2013}}</ref>
After the U.S. withdrawal, the [[:en:JGSDF|JGSDF]] established the Shinodayama Garrison in {{ill|1957|en|1957|WD=Q5311|lt=1957|1=1957}} (Showa 32). The training ground has since been used concurrently with the garrison. From {{ill|1997|en|1997|WD=Q2088|lt=1997|1=1997}} (Heisei 9), the Ministry of Defense began acquiring privately owned land, culminating in the purchase of 16 hectares by the Izumi City Land Development Corporation in {{ill|2004|en|2004|WD=Q2014|lt=2004|1=2004}} (Heisei 16).<ref name="global-kansai"/>
Since {{ill|2003|en|2003|WD=Q1986|lt=2003|1=2003}} (Heisei 15), the {{ill|Shinodayama Cross-Country Race|ja|信太山クロスカントリー大会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinodayama Cross-Country Race|1=Q48755990|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48755990}} has been held at the training ground.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/kakukano/syougaibu/sportssinkou/osirase/kurosukantori_taikai/1321680627212.html|title=History of the Shinodayama Cross-Country Race|publisher=[[Izumi City]]|accessdate=2017-11-09}}</ref>
== Current State == The Shinodayama Hills were once covered in pine forests, but deforestation for fuel and [[:en:pine wilt disease|pine wilt disease]] have reduced vegetation. The soil is highly acidic, making it less suitable for agriculture but ideal for {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} production.<ref name="Izumi City">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/7/02rekishi.pdf|title=Historical Overview of the Shinodayama Hills|publisher=Izumi City|format=PDF|accessdate=2017-11-09}}</ref>
The training ground spans approximately 2.2 million square meters, with about 27,000 square meters of privately owned land. It features numerous reservoirs, including the large Ōtani Reservoir. The area also includes a bypass road with covered firing ranges and remnants of a former vehicle training facility.
The surrounding area is primarily residential, with nearby parks, cemeteries, agricultural fields, and industrial zones. Despite development, the training ground preserves rare natural habitats, leading to conservation efforts by Izumi City and local citizens.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.osaka-izumi.lg.jp/ikkrwebBrowse/material/files/group/7/02gijiroku.pdf|title=2nd Meeting Summary on Shinodayama Hills Conservation|publisher=Izumi City|format=PDF|accessdate=2017-11-09}}</ref>
In the late 1990s, sections of the training ground were affected by illegal industrial waste dumping. Cleanup efforts began in {{ill|2008|en|2008|WD=Q2004|lt=2008|1=2008}} (Heisei 20), funded by the management of Ōtani Reservoir.<ref name="泉州">{{Cite web|url=http://www.sen-shu.com/net/news/news.cgi?mode=print&type=senshu2&no=4545|title=Shinodayama Training Ground Waste Cleanup Begins|publisher=Senshu News|archivedate=2014-02-22}}</ref>
